ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for plain naan; SNOMED CT coverage for specific ethnic flatbreads is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of plain naan weighs approximately 120 g, equivalent to one restaurant-sized portion typically served alongside a North Indian curry or tandoori dish.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Naan — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05030007; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Database food code F05030007 (Plain Naan) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for Indian flatbreads. Trace minerals (copper, manganese) and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18960 (Bread, naan, plain) where HPB data was not available; confidence 0.9 for those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white flour naan; University of Sydney GI Database entry for naan bread (refined wheat flour)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for white flour naan. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (71 × 49.5 × 1.20) / 100 ≈ 42.1 for a 120 g serving. Refined maida flour with minimal fibre content drives the high GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 485 mg per 100 g (582 mg per standard 120 g piece), contributed by salt in the dough, yogurt, and post-bake butter brushing.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit plain naan to half a piece per meal (≤60 g) or substitute with chapati, which contains significantly less sodium; avoid additional salty accompaniments such as pickles or salted butter.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) from refined white flour (maida) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 42 per standard piece — classified as high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict plain naan to half a piece (60 g) or replace it with whole-wheat chapati (GI ~52); pairing with high-protein or high-fibre curries can partially attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content (3.5 g/100 g) from butter/ghee brushing and yogurt, combined with elevated sodium, presents a combined cardiovascular risk at typical restaurant serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit plain naan to one half-piece per meal, request minimal butter brushing, and prioritise whole-grain alternatives to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined flour is nutritionally neutral for inflammation at standard serving levels; small amounts of fat-soluble vitamins (A, E, K) are present from butter brushing.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain naan is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis at standard serving sizes; no specific restriction is required, though whole-grain alternatives offer superior micronutrient density.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain Naan is a classic leavened Indian flatbread made from refined white wheat flour (maida), yogurt, and yeast or baking powder. The dough is hand-stretched into a characteristic teardrop shape and baked at very high heat directly on the inner wall of a clay tandoor oven, producing a bread with golden-brown blistered surfaces, slight char and smokiness on the exterior, and a soft, pillowy, chewy interior. After baking, it is typically brushed with butter or ghee. Per 100 g, plain naan provides approximately 285 kcal, 8 g protein, 6.8 g fat (3.5 g saturated), and 49.5 g carbohydrate with only 1.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 485 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is high (GI 71) due to the refined flour base and low fibre content, making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. Compared to whole-wheat chapati, naan is higher in calories, fat, sodium, and GI, but its superior soft texture makes it the dominant restaurant bread choice across North Indian cuisine. It is vegetarian and halal, and is commonly served alongside curries, dals, and tandoori grills at lunch and dinner in Indian restaurants worldwide.
common_user_queries: naan vs chapati which is healthier

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database food code F05030007 (Plain Naan) with cross-reference to ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables. GI value of 71 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for white flour naan. Ontology codes are defensible and verified. Minor trace minerals (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central with confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for plain naan is not reported in the HPB Foodo Database (F05030007) or ICMR-NIN tables as of 2026-04-13; USDA FoodData Central NDB 18960 does not disaggregate MUFA/PUFA for this product. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo Database for this food code. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed 0.0 — plain naan contains no vitamin C as it is a baked refined flour product with no fruit or vegetable ingredients. Duplicate portions in input (four identical '1 serving (90g)' entries) consolidated to a single default serving of 1 piece (120 g) per HPB verified source. Copper (0.15 mg/100 g), manganese (0.35 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg/100 g) values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central as these were not available in the primary HPB source; confidence 0.9 for these fields.
